channel.title: macOS on notfirefox.dev
channel.description: Recent content in macOS on notfirefox.dev

item.title: Resist the Ecosystem
item.description: We arrived in an age where you have to make a choice. Apple or Google? Android or iOS? Windows or macOS? Either way you are entering not only an operating system but also a whole ecosystem. Every major company strives to provide a whole suite of services and software. If you enter the Apple world you are going to use iCloud, iCloud KeyChain, Apple Mail and so on. Being inside of an ecosystem has advantages.
item.content:encoded:
We arrived in an age where you have to make a choice.

  • Apple or Google?
  • Android or iOS?
  • Windows or macOS?

Either way you are entering not only an operating system but also a whole ecosystem. Every major company strives to provide a whole suite of services and software. If you enter the Apple world you are going to use iCloud, iCloud KeyChain, Apple Mail and so on. Being inside of an ecosystem has advantages. One of them can be security. If all of your eggs are in one basket and that basket has strong security that can be a good thing. Also there is the argument of having less parties to trust. If you decide to use Google services wherever possible you only have one party to trust. But those ecosystems also try to lock you in. Lets say you are deeply into the Apple ecosystem. Thus you are probably using iCloud Keychain to store all of your passwords. iCloud Keychain however only works on iOS, iPadOS, macOS and so on. So using iCloud Keychain on Windows or Android will either not be possible or an unpleasant experience. And this phenomenon happens every time, Apple being the one that locks you in the most aggressively. Ecosystems are nice, I’m not going to deny that. Before we’re looking at the why not we are exploring how to stay out of them.

The first choices to make are the operating systems and hardware choices. My current recommendations would be MicroOS for any desktop device and GrapheneOS on a Google Pixel for a mobile device. There is nothing wrong with using sandboxed Google Play Services and downloading your apps from the Google Play Store, as this also provides better security than F-Droid. So our goal is not necessarily to be completely degoogled. Using the Google Play Store on GrapheneOS does not lure us into the Google ecosystem when compared to the stock Android experience. Furthermore GrapheneOS provides enhanced security when compared to stock Android. I generally recommend avoiding Apple at all cost, I don’t like their vendor lock-in at all.

Now the though part, why do I prefer to stay out of the ecosystem? The obvious answer is that I do so because I can. The point should be made that staying out of the ecosystem requires some amount of effort and being able to make sacrifices, not everyone is able to do that. Then the next question arises if you can stay out, why would you choose to do so. For me personally I just dislike them from the ground up. They want you to subscribe to their services, e.g. Apple One, Google One, Microsoft 365. They integrate everything deeply into their operating system to lure you into their services. I just don’t like that. They have to do that in order to stay profitable and competitive. And while I like that software from big companies sometimes provides greater security, I don’t like to use too much of their software as that lures me into their ecosystem. Occasions where I have chosen software from big companies over free software is Chromium and the Google Play Store.

While the Apple ecosystem is arguably the most expensive one, the Google ecosystem, if existent, is most often thought of as a set of free services like the search engine, Gmail, Google Maps, etc. And I do indeed use some of their services like their search engine, YouTube and Google Maps and they I think they are excellent and I wouldn’t want to miss them. The reason why I’m not investing deeper into the Google ecosystem is diversification. I don’t like making myself dependant on a single company. If there were to happen something to Google I’d have to choose a new search engine, a new maps application, a new website to stream videos and a new place to download my apps from. GrapheneOS and Android are possibly going to survive due to their open source nature.

The point I’m trying to make is, determine which services are really valuable and avoid all other services they provide. Don’t act like big companies don’t exist by degoogling everything at all costs, this may weaken your security unintentionally. Also don’t enter an ecosystem irresponsibly, as this increases your dependency. Depending on a single company for multiple types of information may also be a risk to your privacy, as that company now is a single point of failure. If that company has a security breach or turns out to be not trustworthy, you’re screwed. Having less parties to trust is a double-edged sword.

In the end it’s going to be a personal choice. How much sacrifices are you able to make? What services are absolutely indispensable for you? Depending on your answers, you may not be able to stay out of the ecosystem. Although if you’re not trapped inside of the Apple ecosystem, GrapheneOS is very approachable if you’re willing to use a Google Pixel. Jumping from Windows or macOS to MicroOS is admittedly a bigger step. Switching to MicroOS is potentially the biggest step. But there are a multitude of smaller steps that can be done before which act as a preparation, like switching to an independent email provider or an independent password manager. And for those of you that might be interested in making the switch, there may be more blog posts coming regarding that topic. Just stay tuned.

item.title: My Linux Journey So Far
item.description: My Linux journey began somewhere with Ubuntu. That was back then when Ubuntu was very popular. Later on I made my first experiences with KDE on Kubuntu. Since then I have mostly been using KDE as my main desktop environment. There were times when I tried out Gnome and also window managers like i3 or Sway. But they never stuck. At some point I was fascinated by rolling release distributions like Arch Linux.
item.content:encoded:
My Linux journey began somewhere with Ubuntu. That was back then when Ubuntu was very popular.

Later on I made my first experiences with KDE on Kubuntu. Since then I have mostly been using KDE as my main desktop environment. There were times when I tried out Gnome and also window managers like i3 or Sway. But they never stuck.

At some point I was fascinated by rolling release distributions like Arch Linux. But of course back then there was no archinstall and therefore I had my troubles getting it installed properly. So I tried out Manjaro and the experience wasn’t that bad at all.

And then there was a time where I was using macOS. Yes, I’m being serious. It wasn’t horrible. But it wasn’t good either. I felt so lost on macOS and tried to adopt the macOS way of doing things. The good old window management I have been used to since Windows 7 or Windows XP days is not available on macOS. There is no native alt tabbing on macOS. You swipe up with three fingers on your touchpad and suddenly all of your applications appear in a unorganized way on your screen. If you have multiple instances of a program open and click on the icon in the dock it opens up all of them. No way to select which one you want right now. Window management was downright bad for my preferred way of using a computer. And the macOS way of doing things did not feel efficient in any way compared to my old ways of doing things. Therefore I switched again.

Enter Arch Linux. I have been very happy on Arch. But I wanted to try out openSUSE Tumbleweed. So I did. And openSUSE Tumbleweed is amazing. I am still not sure which of both I like better. But it’s probably Tumbleweed. Though I have to say pacman is so much faster than zypper. But the experience on Arch is generally so much more DIY. I liked that. But I also like the fact that the Tumbleweed installation by itself felt more stable for no apparent reason. It just feels like a polished product. Whereas Arch always feels incomplete to me.

With the switch to Tumbleweed yet another transition took place. I got into the openSUSE ecosystem. I also thought about using openSUSE Leap. But I stayed with the rolling release philosophy. My last transition should introduce something completely new. And that is called an immutable OS. I am certainly not an expert on that topic so don’t quote me on the following. What differentiates it from regular distributions is that the root filesystem is mounted as read only. This prevents malicious or accidental modifications to the operating system. Changes to the running operating system are not permitted. Changes are applied to a snapshot that will be booted on the next boot. Automatic checks are applied to determine if the snapshot is functional, otherwise the previous snapshot will be loaded. Thus the host OS is able to update in an automated fashion and rollback by itself if something goes wrong. The host system is separated and supposed to stay small. Applications are preferably installed using Flatpak. Development environments can be set up using Distrobox.

It’s been a long way. But I’m finally fairly happy where I’m currently at. I want to use technology that I can also recommend to other people. And I would generally not recommend anything based on Debian such as Ubuntu or Kubuntu. Manjaro has its own issues. Arch Linux is not usable for most people. Tumbleweed is awesome. But I don’t know if I would recommend Tumbleweed to regular people. Managing packages is not something regular people want to do. And graphical frontends more often than not failed for me when trying to update the system. This is due to the fact that e.g. packages might change vendor when you have the packman repo enabled. This currently cannot be handled by Discover, which is the graphical frontend for KDE. MicroOS can update itself. Not only that it can also determine by itself if the update has been successful. If the checks consider the update faulty they will automatically rollback to the previous snapshot. Programs like web browsers and user focused programs can simply be installed as Flatpaks using Discover and that works reliably.

Advanced features are available using containers. I am running Arch Linux inside of my MicroOS installation using Distrobox. So every time I run nvim, hugo or other CLI applications it actually utilizes the applications installed inside of my Distrobox. Why is that useful? Arch Linux has a lot of development packages available directly in the official repositories such as bash-language-server, gopls, github-cli. This saves me the hassle of trying to figure out how to install a language server I need. On Arch I just know it works. Also those packages are installed inside of an user Distrobox. That means that they are not part of the host OS. And to me it kind of makes sense to put those CLI applications inside of a separate development environment. They don’t need to be part of the core OS.

Lets wrap it up. I think that MicroOS is a promising attempt to make Linux more accessible to more people. But it also makes the process of managing a system less of a hassle. Updates are going to work. I know that. If they don’t the system will rollback for me. Applications are installed via Flatpak. The development environment sits inside of an Arch Linux container. I am surprised how easy the transition from Tumbleweed to MicroOS actually was. And although the MicroOS Desktop KDE variant is currently officially still in alpha state, it feels just as solid if not even more so than Tumbleweed. But it’s not quite polished yet, e.g. I expected kcm_sddm to be preinstalled on KDE. Going on forward I am convinced that immutable operating systems and especially MicroOS are the way to go!
